rights: You must provide license, registration, and proof of insurance when requested
You have the right to remain silent beyond identifying yourself
You can refuse to answer questions about where you are going or coming from
You can refuse consent to search your vehicle (say clearly: 'I do not consent to a search')
Police may order you and passengers out of the vehicle (Pennsylvania v. Mimms)
Police may conduct a pat-down if they have reasonable suspicion you are armed (Terry v. Ohio)
Police cannot extend the stop beyond the time needed for its purpose without reasonable suspicion (Rodriguez v. United States)
You have the right to record the interaction (1st Amendment in most jurisdictions)
what_to_do: Pull over safely and turn on interior lights at night
Keep hands visible on the steering wheel
Be polite but exercise your rights
Clearly state 'I do not consent to a search' if asked
You may ask 'Am I free to go?' to determine if you are being detained
Inform the officer if you need to reach for documents
Provide license and registration when requested
what_not_to_do: Do not reach for anything without telling the officer first
Do not argue, resist, or obstruct even if you believe the stop is unlawful
Do not flee from the stop
Do not consent to a search of your vehicle or person
Do not lie or provide false identification
Do not make sudden movements
traffic_stop.when_to_invoke_counsel: If you are arrested or if questioning goes beyond a routine traffic stop, clearly state: 'I want to speak with an attorney before answering any questions.'

constitutional_basis: 4th Amendment (arrest must be based on probable cause)
rights: You have the right to remain silent (Miranda v. Arizona)
Anything you say can and will be used against you in court
You have the right to an attorney
If you cannot afford an attorney, one will be appointed for you
You have the right to know the charges against you
You have the right to make a phone call (timing varies by jurisdiction)
You have the right to be brought before a judge within a reasonable time (typically 48-72 hours)
You have the right to bail in most non-capital cases (8th Amendment)
what_to_do: Clearly state: 'I invoke my right to remain silent'
Clearly state: 'I want an attorney'
Comply with the physical arrest (do not resist)
Remember officer names, badge numbers, and patrol car numbers
Note the time and circumstances of the arrest
Remain calm and polite
Make your phone call to an attorney or family member
what_not_to_do: Do NOT resist arrest, even if you believe it is unlawful
Do NOT answer questions after invoking your right to silence
Do NOT sign anything without your attorney present
Do NOT consent to any searches
Do NOT discuss your case with cellmates or on jail phones (calls are recorded)
Do NOT make statements 'off the record' (there is no such thing)
arrest.when_to_invoke_counsel: Immediately upon arrest. Say clearly: 'I am invoking my right to remain silent and I want to speak with an attorney.' Then stop talking.

search.situation: Search of Person, Home, or Property
constitutional_basis: 4th Amendment (protection against unreasonable searches and seizures)
rights: Police generally need a warrant to search your home (with exceptions)
You can refuse consent to a search of your home, car, or person
If police have a warrant, you have the right to see it and verify its scope
Police may search incident to a lawful arrest (Chimel v. California)
The 'plain view' doctrine allows seizure of contraband in plain sight during lawful presence
Exigent circumstances may justify a warrantless search (imminent destruction of evidence, hot pursuit)
You have the right to observe the search if present
Items seized outside the warrant's scope may be suppressible
what_to_do: Clearly state: 'I do not consent to this search'
Ask to see the warrant and note what it authorizes (specific location, items)
Do not physically interfere with the search, even if you believe it is unlawful
Take mental notes of what is searched and seized
Ask for a receipt/inventory of items seized
Contact an attorney as soon as possible
what_not_to_do: Do NOT physically resist or obstruct the search
Do NOT destroy, hide, or discard evidence during the search
Do NOT give consent even if pressured (they can search without your consent if they have a warrant)
Do NOT answer questions during the search
search.when_to_invoke_counsel: As soon as you are aware a search is being conducted. State: 'I do not consent to this search and I want to speak with my attorney.'

rights: You have the right to remain silent at all times
Miranda warnings must be given before custodial interrogation
You can invoke your right to silence at any time during questioning
You can request an attorney at any time, and questioning must stop (Edwards v. Arizona)
Once you invoke your right to counsel, police cannot reinitiate questioning without your attorney
Voluntary statements made before Miranda warnings may still be used (in some circumstances)
You cannot be punished for invoking your right to remain silent
Confessions obtained through coercion are inadmissible
what_to_do: Clearly and unambiguously invoke your rights: 'I want a lawyer' (not 'Maybe I should get a lawyer')
After invoking, remain completely silent
Do not engage in small talk with officers (it can be used against you)
Request that questioning stop immediately
Wait for your attorney before answering any questions
what_not_to_do: Do NOT waive your Miranda rights
Do NOT agree to 'just tell your side of the story'
Do NOT believe promises of leniency from interrogators
Do NOT try to outsmart the interrogators
Do NOT make partial admissions thinking they will help you
Do NOT take a polygraph (lie detector) test without attorney advice
Do NOT answer 'just a few questions' without your lawyer
interrogation.when_to_invoke_counsel: Before answering ANY substantive questions. The magic words are: 'I invoke my right to remain silent. I want a lawyer.' Then say nothing more.

rights: You have the right to peacefully assemble and protest on public property
Counter-protesters have the same rights you do
You can photograph and record police and public events in public spaces
Police cannot prevent you from protesting based on viewpoint
You may need a permit for certain activities (large gatherings, amplified sound, street closures)
Private property owners can restrict protest on their property
You have the right to distribute literature and display signs in public
Police must give clear dispersal orders before declaring an unlawful assembly
what_to_do: Know the local permit requirements beforehand
Write the number of a legal aid organization on your arm
Carry government-issued ID
Keep your phone charged and have an emergency contact
Stay on public property (sidewalks, parks, plazas)
Document any interactions with police
Follow lawful police orders (including lawful dispersal orders)
Stay aware of your surroundings
what_not_to_do: Do NOT engage in violence or property destruction (not protected speech)
Do NOT block traffic or emergency vehicles unless permitted
Do NOT trespass on private property
Do NOT resist arrest if you are detained
Do NOT bring weapons
Do NOT make threats (true threats are not protected by the 1st Amendment)
protest.when_to_invoke_counsel: If you are detained or arrested during a protest, immediately state: 'I want an attorney.' Do not discuss the protest or your activities with police.

constitutional_basis: 6th Amendment (right to counsel, though limited in grand jury)
rights: You can invoke the 5th Amendment and refuse to answer questions that might incriminate you
You do not have the right to have an attorney present IN the grand jury room (federal), but you can step outside to consult
You must appear if subpoenaed, but you can assert privilege on specific questions
Grand jury proceedings are secret (Rule 6(e), FRCP)
You cannot be compelled to testify if granted immunity and the testimony could still incriminate you under state law
If granted use immunity, you must testify (your testimony cannot be used against you, but other evidence can)
what_to_do: Retain an attorney immediately upon receiving a subpoena
Discuss potential 5th Amendment invocations with your attorney before appearing
You may step out of the grand jury room to consult with your attorney between questions
Be truthful if you do answer (lying to a grand jury is perjury)
what_not_to_do: Do NOT ignore a grand jury subpoena (contempt of court)
Do NOT discuss your testimony with anyone other than your attorney
Do NOT lie or make false statements
Do NOT destroy documents or evidence covered by the subpoena
grand_jury.when_to_invoke_counsel: Immediately upon receiving a grand jury subpoena. An attorney can help you understand your rights, prepare for testimony, and identify questions you may refuse to answer.
